answer:
**In the Old English poem "Widsith" (chapter 14 of *Old English Elegies*), the narrator—a wandering scop named Widsith—claims to have traveled far and wide, serving many lords and visiting numerous tribes and their rulers.**[^1]

Three kings he specifically names as having visited include:

- **Eormanric**, king of the Goths (also called the Hreth-king), with whom he stayed for a long time and from whom he received a valuable gold ring.[^2]
- **Guthere**, king of the Burgundians, who rewarded his song with a gleaming treasure (a ring).[^3]
- **Ælfwine**, son of Eadwine, whom he visited in Italy and praises for generosity with rings and praise.[^4]

Widsith recites an even longer catalogue of over 70 rulers and tribes he claims association with across Europe, Asia, and biblical lands, blending historical, legendary, and mythical figures.[^5]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14 ("Widsith matholade, wordhord onleac"), translation, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, translation, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-f8162a).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, translation, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-8276c0).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, translation, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-8276c0).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, translation, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-545201).
